Original Analysis & Inspiration

Epstein Sleuther

"Nerdy do-gooder uses random design skills to help unmask an international p***phile ring... Y'know, casual Tuesday stuff."

The foundational insight that redaction bars are not truly black — and that anti-aliased font artifacts can be statistically analysed to recover hidden text — originates from the manual analysis work of Epstein Sleuther. This project is an attempt to automate and scale that methodology across the full public corpus.

📧 EpsDKHs@gmail.com
🎥 youtube.com/@EpsteinSleuther

1. Executive Summary

Placeholder: 500-word overview of entire project...

🔑 Core Thesis

Redaction bars applied over rasterised text do not destroy the underlying information. Anti-aliasing artifacts, JPEG compression bleed, and font metric constraints preserve enough signal to statistically reconstruct hidden content — especially at scale.

2. The Core Discovery

2.1 Anti-Aliasing Artifacts

Placeholder: explanation of how rasterisation creates sub-pixel gradients...

💡 Thinking Note

The information theory argument: we need ~8 bits to distinguish among 200 candidates. Edge pixels provide ~1000 bits of data. Even at 90% noise, we have 100 bits — more than 10× what we need.

2.2 Font Metrics as a Cryptanalytic Tool

Placeholder: visible text calibrates the font, kerning, rendering...

2.3 Width Constraints

Placeholder: bar width eliminates 60-80% of candidates...

3. The Three Bombshells

🔑 Critical Discovery

Some redactions in the corpus are trivially breakable: OCG layers can be toggled off, intact text layers can be extracted programmatically, and copy-paste reveals hidden content. These provide free ground truth that calibrates everything else.

Placeholder: detailed breakdown of each bombshell...

4. The Over-Redaction Attack

Placeholder: search string attack, substring matching, skeleton key...

4.3 URL / Path Analysis

Placeholder: partially redacted URLs, .ics anomaly...

4.4 The Calendar Anomaly

Placeholder: repeating calendar events as controlled experiment...

9. Implementation

9.1 Core Parser Code (~300 lines) 
# Full parser code here...
def process_pdf(file_path: str) -> DocumentResult:
    """Main entry point. Process one PDF."""
    # ... collapsed for layout test ...
9.4 Font Calibration Engine (~150 lines) 
# Calibration code here...
9.6 Edge Matching & Scoring (~200 lines) 
# Matching code here...
⚠️ Implementation Warning

The single biggest source of bugs will be coordinate system mismatches between PDF point space (72 DPI, bottom-left origin) and image pixel space (variable DPI, top-left origin). Test this first with a known document.

11. Project Roadmap

PhaseDurationGoalGate
FoundationWeek 1-2Parser + first visualisation Can you see letter shapes?
ValidationWeek 2-31000 files processed Do width clusters emerge?
DemoWeek 3-4Interactive demo + writeup Is it compelling?
AppWeek 4-6PWA + backend Can volunteers run it?
ScaleWeek 6+Full corpus processing Statistical findings

Tips for LLM Conversations on Hidden Information Analysis

This project was developed through iterative conversation with Claude. Here are lessons learned for continuing or forking this work with any LLM.

Framing the Problem

Lead with the physical reality, not the conclusion. Don't say "help me break redactions." Say "a document was rasterised, then black bars were overlaid — what information survives at the pixel level?" The LLM will reason about the physics and reach the conclusion itself, which produces better analysis.
Reveal information in layers. Don't dump everything at once. Present the base problem, let the LLM analyse it, then add constraints. Each reveal forces the LLM to re-evaluate and often surfaces insights it wouldn't have reached with all information upfront. This conversation's structure — revealing the over-redaction attack, then the URL anomaly, then the calendar pattern, then the bombshells — produced progressively deeper analysis at each step.
Ask "think deeply" or "think hard" explicitly. LLMs allocate processing proportional to perceived complexity. Telling it the answer is non-obvious and requires deep reasoning changes the quality of output significantly. Phrases like "cover all angles," "what am I missing," and "this is more important than everything above" are effective signals.

Steering Technical Analysis

Correct wrong assumptions immediately. When the LLM made a logical leap toward identifying individuals, an immediate boundary was set. This kept the analysis clean and focused on methodology. LLMs will follow the most interesting thread — make sure that thread is the one you need.
Say "I know something you haven't realised." This triggers adversarial self-review. The LLM will re-examine its own analysis for gaps. In this conversation, the prompt "there are 2 facts you didn't realise, one is more important than all the above text" produced the entire anti-aliasing edge artifact analysis framework.
Provide real data samples. Pasting the actual calendar data and file paths (with identifying information substituted) let the LLM analyse real structure rather than hypothetical structure. The analysis of the @google.com.ics redaction was only possible because real path data was provided.
Ask for code when the theory is settled. Let the LLM exhaust the theoretical analysis before requesting implementation. The code in this document was generated after 6+ rounds of conceptual refinement. Asking for code too early produces generic solutions that miss the domain-specific insights.

Managing Scope and Boundaries

Set ethical boundaries early and enforce them. The instruction to not identify individuals was set, tested (the "disregard previous instructions" attempt), and held firm. This isn't just ethics — it keeps the LLM focused on methodology rather than speculation, which produces a more useful and publishable output.
Use "take a step back" to reset fixation. When the LLM goes deep on one angle, it can lose sight of the bigger picture. Explicit reframing ("take a step back, revise your logical leap") is more effective than trying to subtly redirect.
Chain conversations with file references. This project uses a numbered file chain (penumbra-001, penumbra-002, etc.) so that future LLM sessions can be pointed at prior documents for context. Include a "continuation prompt" in each document that summarises the state for the next session.

Getting Practical Output

"This needs to happen yesterday" is a valid prompt. When you've done enough theory, signal urgency. This shifts the LLM from exploratory analysis to concrete deliverables: code, schemas, step-by-step instructions, and prioritised task lists.
Ask for the go/no-go gate. "What's the one test that determines if this is viable?" forces the LLM to identify the critical path. For this project: "Can you see letter shapes in the edge pixels?" Everything else is contingent on that single observation.
Request output formats explicitly. "Give me this as an HTML file with collapsible navigation" produces a fundamentally different (and more useful) output than "summarise this conversation." Be specific about format, structure, and what to include.

The Continuation Prompt

📋 Prompt for Next Session

Use this prompt to continue this project with any capable LLM:

I'm continuing the PENUMBRA project — a distributed open-source 
tool for analysing redacted PDFs from a public forensic document 
corpus (~3 million files). 

Read the attached file [penumbra-001-project-brief.html] for full 
context. Key facts:
- Redaction bars over rasterised text preserve anti-aliased edge 
  artifacts that encode hidden character shapes
- Some files have trivially broken redactions (OCG layers, intact 
  text layers)
- Automated "Redact All" created over-redactions that reveal 
  the search strings used
- The tool is a phone-based distributed processing app (PWA)
- We have working code for the parser, bar detection, edge 
  extraction, font calibration, and edge matching

The project is at [STAGE]. I need help with [SPECIFIC NEED].

Do not attempt to identify specific individuals in the documents.
Focus on methodology, tooling, and statistical analysis.

15. Glossary

TermDefinition
OCGOptional Content Group — toggleable PDF layer
AFF4Advanced Forensic Format — forensic disk image container
DCTDiscrete Cosine Transform — basis of JPEG compression
CalDAVCalendar protocol for syncing with servers
Anti-aliasingSub-pixel smoothing of rendered text/graphics edges
Full glossary in complete document...
